Prompt Engineering Techniques to Leverage AI


How to Develop Powerful Prompts and Become a Prompt Successful Professional Maker

Author's Note

We live in an era where artificial intelligence is advancing rapidly, transforming the world and our daily lives. However, even with all these technological advances, one essential skill still remains in human hands: the ability to ask the right questions. Being a " Prompt " Maker is more than just interacting with AI models; it is knowing how to guide these machines in order to extract their full potential. Through this ebook , I want to show the importance of Prompt Engineering , a fundamental skill, even in times of advanced AI. The ability to structure questions in an organized and creative way allows us to command artificial intelligence towards the desired results, maximizing the value of the answers obtained.

Master of Prompts

Preface

Welcome to the fascinating world of Prompt Engineering ! As artificial intelligence becomes more and more present in our lives, understanding how to interact with these models effectively has become an essential skill. The profession of " Prompt Maker " is the answer for those who want to enhance their interactions with AI, creating questions in a meticulous and structured way. This ebook is a guide for all those who want to master this skill, ranging from the most basic to the most advanced techniques. Here you will learn how to formulate the best prompts , expanding your possibilities and making the most of the capabilities of these intelligences. I invite you to explore these techniques and empower yourself for this new future that opens up before us.

Full Summary

1. Role Play

2. Chain of Thought (Chain of Thoughts)

3. Zero- shot Prompting ( Zero Examples Prompt )

4. Few-shot Prompting ( Few Example Prompt )

5. Self- Reflective Prompting ( Model Self-Reflection )

6. Instruction-based Prompting ( Instruction-Based Prompting )

7. Reframing

8. Context Expansion (Context Expansion)

9. Elimination

10. Goal Decomposition (Objective Decomposition)

11. Step-by-Step Detailing (Step-by-Step Detailing)

12. Feedback Loop Prompting

13. Iterative Refinement (Iterative Refinement)

14. Hypothetical Reasoning (Hypothetical Reasoning)

15. Comparative Prompting ( Comparative Prompt )

16. Scenario Planning

17. Role Reversal Prompting (Role Reversal)

18. Consensus Building Prompting (Consensus Building)

19. Contrarian Prompting ( Contrary Prompt )

20. Step-by-Step Analysis (Step-by-Step Analysis)

21. Devil's Advocate Prompting (Devil's Advocate)

22. Scenario Comparison (Scenario Comparison)

23. Problem Breakdown (Problem Decomposition)

24. Iterative Brainstorming

25. Perspective Switching

Introduction to Prompt Engineering and the Promptmaker
Profession Prompt Engineering is an emerging field that has gained momentum along with the advancement of artificial intelligence, especially with the development of LLMs ( Large Language Models ). What we often fail to realize is that the true potential of these AIs lies in how we frame the questions. Prompts are, in simple terms, the instructions we give the AI to provide an answer. The quality of the prompts determines the quality of the answers, which is why the ability to create good prompts is critical.

A “ Promptmaker ” is someone who is able to translate a need into an effective interaction with AI . Being a promptmaker means having a creative and analytical mind, knowing how to guide the model to achieve the desired result. This profession becomes increasingly important as we advance in the use of AIs as assistants, teachers, counselors, and so many other roles. Knowing how to interact appropriately with these machines is what differentiates a simple experience from a transformative and value-rich experience.

Even in a context of increasing automation, the human ability to formulate good questions and guide artificial intelligence is what makes them truly effective. No matter how advanced the AI model is, if the instructions provided are not adequate, the potential of the system will be limited. Therefore, Prompt Engineering is much more than a technical skill; it is the key that puts us in control of AI, maximizing its value and its impact on problem-solving, innovation and creativity.

1. Role Play

[What is it ]
The Role Play technique, or Role Game, is a strategy where the LLM (language model) is instructed to assume a specific role, as if it were a character with defined characteristics. In this type of prompt , the objective is to define the context and characteristics of the model's “persona”, to directly influence the type of response it will provide.

For example, the LLM may be asked to act as a history teacher, a culinary expert, an investigative journalist, or even a travel assistant. This allows the model to provide more contextualized responses that are relevant to the specific role they have been given.

[What is your main goal ?]
The main goal of the Role Play technique is to guide the model's response generation towards a certain tone or perspective, resulting in information that is more useful and specific to a particular context. By giving the model a role, it becomes easier to obtain an output that is consistent with the user's expectations.

Furthermore, the use of this technique facilitates problem-solving or discussions in specific areas, since the model will be simulating the knowledge or behavior of an expert on the subject, making the response richer and more aligned with the role played.

[How it works ]
Role Play works by placing the LLM in a specific context, where it assumes a persona defined by the user. The prompt should begin by explaining to the model that it now has a role and provide some guidelines on what is expected of that role. The more detailed the instructions, the more specific the response will be.

For example, when asking LLM to behave like a nutritionist, the user can define characteristics such as “you are a nutritionist specializing in vegetarian diets”. This already restricts the scope of the model’s responses and makes the suggestions given by LLM aligned with the specific knowledge of this area.

The technique also works by allowing the use of language specific to that role. A character can use jargon or expressions typical of the profession or context in which they are inserted, which increases the immersion and relevance of the response. This is particularly useful in situations where formality, expertise or even empathy are important.

[When to use ]
Role Play is especially useful when you need a highly contextualized response and need to ensure that the LLM responds in a specific way. Whether you are developing educational content, providing medical advice, or simply want to test different perspectives on a question, Role Play is an excellent technique.

This technique is ideal in situations where you want to obtain expert answers without having to provide explicit examples. If the question involves a particular scenario, such as a conversation between a patient and a doctor, or when you are trying to simulate an interview with a celebrity, Role Play can ensure that the answer is contextualized and coherent.

It can also be used when a specific emotion needs to be conveyed, such as a motivational counselor. If the LLM needs to provide emotional support or more empathetic responses, placing them in a counselor or psychologist role can be an effective way to shape the response.

[Tips for use]

●        Be clear about what role the role model is supposed to play, and provide specific characteristics that define that role. For example, “You are a science teacher specializing in ecology.”

●        Including examples of the type of behavior expected is good practice. For example, “use simple language, speak to elementary school students.”

●        Provide context about the scenario in which this role is being played , so the answer will be even more accurate and contextualized.

[The difference in the technique ]
The Role Play technique differs from other prompting techniques because it focuses on shaping the LLM's behavior as a whole, rather than providing a specific set of examples. It can be a powerful way to elicit responses that are consistent with a particular persona, without having to provide examples of every type of question and answer.

Compared to Few-shot Prompting , which provides sample inputs and outputs to model the LLM's response, Role Play is more of a configuration of the model's "mental state", allowing it to adapt more broadly and quickly to the desired context. By providing a specific role to the LLM, you are defining how the model should process the information and the perspective it should have.

Role Play is also more straightforward than techniques that require decomposing goals or breaking them down into steps, since all of the definition of the desired behavior happens at the initial prompt . This makes it a practical and versatile tool for a wide variety of scenarios.

[Examples]

●        Basic : "You are a history teacher. Explain to me how the French Revolution started. "

●        Intermediate : "You are a nutritionist specializing in vegan diets . What foods are most recommended to increase protein intake? "

●        Advanced : "You are a senior software developer working at an artificial intelligence startup . Explain the pros and cons of using Python versus Java in a machine learning project."

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Structured Role Play Prompt :

○        {"You are" ["Define the role of the role model, e.g. a teacher, nutritionist, specialist"] }

○        {"Specialized in" ["If there is a specific area of expertise, e.g. vegan nutrition , ecology"] }

○        {"Its purpose is to" ["Describe the goal of the model, e.g.: provide a detailed explanation, nutritional advice"] }

○        {"Use" ["Language style or tone, e.g., plain language, empathetic approach"] }

○        Complete Example : "You are a scientist specializing in climate change. Explain in simple terms how the greenhouse effect affects the planet. Use language that is accessible to high school students. "

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Ask the model to act as a tour guide explaining a famous attraction in your city. Define the attraction and the features you would like to see covered.
  2. Instruct the LLM to be a doctor explaining the risks of a sedentary lifestyle to a lay patient, focusing on providing practical and accessible advice.
  3. Create a prompt where the LLM should act as a child psychologist helping a child deal with a fear of the dark. Define what the tone of the conversation and approach should be.

2. Chain of Thought (Chain of Thoughts)

[What is ]
Chain of Thought , or Chain of Thoughts, is a prompting engineering technique that instructs the LLM to "think out loud" as it comes to a conclusion. Instead of giving a direct answer, the model must first describe the steps or reasoning that led it to that answer. This creates a sort of chain of reasoning, where the model lays out each step until it reaches the conclusion.

This technique is useful for making reasoning more transparent and understandable. The model works as if it were "thinking" and explaining each decision made throughout the process, which is especially useful for complex questions, mathematical problems, or logical dilemmas.

[What is your main objective ]
The main objective of the Chain of Thought is about guiding the model towards a logical and coherent answer, giving visibility to the reasoning steps. Often, a direct answer can omit important information, which leads to an incomplete or incorrect result. The Thought Chain aims to ensure that each step that led to the answer is clearly detailed and explained.

Another important goal is to use the LLM’s ability to solve complex problems more effectively. Asking the model to describe its process helps avoid answers that may seem correct but are actually wrong due to a lack of clear logic. This technique encourages the model to check itself at each step, increasing the accuracy of its answers.

[How it works ]
Chain of Thought works by explicitly asking the model to think in steps. The prompt should be constructed in a way that makes it clear that the LLM should explain each part of the process until it reaches the final result. This is often done through keywords such as “explain step by step,” “describe the process,” or “think through each step before giving the answer.”

This technique is powerful because LLMs have difficulty maintaining consistent reasoning when unguided. By breaking the task into smaller steps, it becomes easier for the model to process information, apply rules, and avoid shortcuts that could compromise the answer. The Chain of Thought not only increases transparency, but also improves the quality of the answers.

Furthermore, the Chain of Thought can help detect potential errors. By presenting a step-by-step process, the user can check and understand where, if any, a flaw in the logic occurred. This allows for more accurate and effective correction, helping with learning or problem-solving.

[When to use ]
Chain of Thought is ideal in situations where the problem has several logical steps or where the answer requires detailed explanation to be understood. For example, problems in mathematics, logic, text analysis, or any situation that involves detailed planning benefit greatly from this approach.

The technique should be used whenever it is necessary to obtain a clear understanding of the intermediate steps to reach the final solution. In educational contexts, for example, Chain of Thought is great for teaching students step-by-step processes, showing not just the answer, but how it was achieved.

In complex analysis or diagnostic situations, the Chain of Thought is also extremely useful. Imagine using the model to analyze a medical situation: by explaining each step of the diagnosis, it is possible to ensure that all factors are considered and understood, making the answer much more valuable.

[Tips for use]

●        Use words that encourage the model to detail their steps, such as "think step by step," "explain each phase," or "list each step."

●        If you need a very detailed answer, be sure to instruct the model not to skip steps, even if they seem obvious.

[The difference of the technique ]
Chain of Thought Prompt differs from more direct techniques such as Zero- shot or Role Play in that it focuses on the thought process. While other prompts aim to elicit an immediate answer, Thought Prompt seeks to understand the "how" and " why " of the answer by breaking it down into sequential, easily understandable steps.

Compared to Few-shot Prompting , where examples are given for the model, the Chain of Thought uses each step of reasoning incrementally, which not only improves understanding but also makes it easier for the user to learn about the structure of the problem. Few-shot can show how to do something, but Chain of Thought really explains the process.

Chain of Thought is therefore a deeper and more analytical technique, helping both in developing the answer and in providing clarity for the user. By showing how the model arrives at the answer, this approach ends up being more reliable and less prone to errors.

[Examples]

●        Basic : "What is the sum of the numbers 1 to 5? Think step by step before answering. "

○        Answer : "First, we add 1 + 2 = 3. Then, 3 + 3 = 6. Next, 6 + 4 = 10. Finally, 10 + 5 = 15. Therefore, the answer is 15. "

●        Intermediate : "How do you solve the problem of 12 divided by 4 , and then subtract 3 from the result? Explain each step."

○        Answer : "First, let's divide 12 by 4 , which equals 3. Then we subtract 3 from the result, and that equals 0."

●        Advanced : "Explain how to solve the equation 2x + 3 = 11. Think about each step until you reach the value of x. "

○        Answer : "First, let's subtract 3 from both sides of the equation: 2x + 3 - 3 = 11 - 3, resulting in 2x = 8. After that, we divide both sides by 2, so x = 4."

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Chain of Thought Structured Prompt :

○        {"Explain the reasoning" ["Detail that should be thought about step by step"]}

○        {"Describe each step" ["Instruct the model to explain all stages of the process, e.g. 1 through 5"]}

○        {"Think before you answer" ["Reinforce the need to analyze the problem in parts"]}

○        Complete Example : "How do you solve the problem 5 x 4 + 3? Think about each step, starting with multiplication and then moving on to addition."

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Ask the model to calculate the sum of all odd numbers between 1 and 10, explaining each step of the process.
  2. Instruct the model to explain how to solve the mathematical expression 3x - 5 = 10. Make sure the model thinks through each step before providing the answer.
  3. Ask the model how to organize a party for 10 people, including shopping list, venue planning, and preparation. Ask him or her to describe each step in detail.

3. Zero- shot Prompting ( Zero Examples Prompt )

[What is ]
Zero - shot Prompting is a prompt engineering technique that involves asking the LLM to generate a response without providing any prior examples. The idea is that the model responds based on its general training and natural language understanding, without needing specific samples to know what type of response to expect.

The term "Zero- shot " refers to the fact that no detailed instructions or previous examples are provided for the model to follow, only a question or task is given. This technique takes advantage of the LLM's ability to understand and process language without additional context.

[What is your main objective ]
The main objective of Zero- shot Prompting is testing the model's ability to respond accurately to a request using only its general knowledge. It is especially useful when the user wants to get quick, uncomplicated responses or when the goal is to assess the model's competence without extra help.

Another objective of Zero- shot Prompting is about simplifying the prompting process by eliminating the need to think through detailed examples or complex contextualizations. This can be useful for more straightforward questions or for getting a first impression of the model's understanding of a particular topic.

[How it works ]
Zero- shot Prompting works by simply asking a question or providing a task directly to the model, without any examples or context beyond the prompt itself. The LLM uses its prior knowledge to interpret and respond to the prompt, based on the patterns it learned during its training.

Since the LLM is not given examples, it needs to understand the structure of the question, the key words, and what is expected of it from the general meaning. This requires the question to be asked in a clear and straightforward manner, as there are no additional instructions to help the model "better understand" what is expected.

The lack of examples makes Zero- shot Prompting is a less specific technique than others, but it can be effective when used with simple, straightforward prompts . Because LLM is trained on a vast amount of data, it can often provide a relevant answer even without extra guidance. This technique is best suited for situations where extreme precision or detail is not required.

[When to use]
Zero- shot Prompting should be used when you need a quick and simple answer. If the question is straightforward and the context is easily understandable to anyone, this technique can be extremely effective and practical.

It is also a good technique to use when you want to test the model's ability to understand a request without additional help. If you want to know how well LLM is able to answer new or little-explored questions, Zero- shot Prompting can provide an initial perspective.

Additionally, it is useful in situations where the user does not want or need to waste time creating specific examples, such as for common everyday questions, general information or brief descriptions of concepts.

[Tips for use]

●        Be specific and clear when formulating the prompt , as the LLM will not be provided with examples to help you. Try to avoid ambiguity.

●        Prefer to use this technique for questions or requests where the answer is objective, as the lack of examples can make it difficult for the LLM to give a very precise or detailed answer.

[The difference of the technique ]
Zero- shot Prompting is different from techniques like Few-shot Prompting , where specific examples are provided to help the model understand the format or type of response expected. In Zero- shot , the LLM's ability to generalize knowledge is tested, as it is not given additional context.

Compared to Chain of Thought , where the LLM is instructed to explain the reasoning step by step, Zero- shot Prompting just asks directly and waits for a response. While Chain of Thought is concerned with the process, Zero- shot focuses only on the result, in a direct way.

It is also different from Role Play in that, in Zero- shot , the LLM is not instructed to assume a specific persona or context. There is no narrative or role that needs to be followed, which makes the response more generic and potentially less detailed.

[Examples]

●        Basic : "What is photosynthesis? "

○        Answer : "Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into oxygen and glucose. "

●        Intermediate : "What are the main ingredients for making homemade bread? "

○        Answer : "The main ingredients are flour, water, yeast and salt. "

●        Advanced : "What is the importance of Einstein's theory of relativity? "

○        Answer : "Einstein's theory of relativity describes how time and space are interconnected and how gravity affects both, changing our understanding of the universe and having important applications in technologies such as GPS."

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Zero- shot Structured Prompt :

○        {"Direct Question" ["Provide a direct question without additional context"]}

○        {"Don't provide examples" ["No need to provide examples or extra context"]}

○        Complete Example : "What is a solar eclipse? "

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Ask the model a simple question, such as "What is a shooting star?" and evaluate the quality of the answer provided without additional examples.
  2. Ask the model "What is the capital of France?" and see if it can answer correctly without the need for context.
  3. Formulate a more complex question, such as "What is the function of the nucleus in a eukaryotic cell?" and see if the LLM provides a relevant explanation without examples.

4. Few-shot Prompting ( Few Example Prompt )

[What is ]
Few-shot Prompting is a prompt engineering technique that consists of providing the LLM with some sample questions and answers before asking it to generate a response for a new case. The examples provided serve to guide the model on what type of response to expect and what format is ideal. These examples act as a guide, helping the LLM understand the pattern and adapt its responses accordingly.

This technique is especially useful for guiding the LLM through tasks that are nuanced or require a specific response, without having to provide lengthy instructions. By providing appropriate examples, you increase the accuracy and relevance of the LLM’s response.

[What is your main objective ]
The main objective of Few-shot Prompting is improving the quality of the LLM response by providing some examples that set the tone, format, and type of response that is expected. This way, even if the initial request is more complex, the model will have a more solid basis for formulating its responses.

Another important goal is to reduce response ambiguity. When the model is given examples, it has a better understanding of the type of approach and style it should follow. This helps minimize incorrect responses or responses that fall outside the intended scope. This is especially effective for tasks that require a certain formatting pattern, such as lists, summaries, or dialogues.

[How it works ]
Few-shot Prompting works by including some examples before the main prompt. These examples are like a “preview” for the LLM, which then better understands the context and the style it should apply. These examples usually include both the question and the answer, making it clear to the model how it should behave.

The examples provided should be representative of the type of task being asked. For example, if you want the model to generate short, to-the-point explanations, the examples should also be short and to the point. The LLM uses these examples to establish a pattern and then generate a response that follows the same format and style.

An important feature of Few-shot Prompting is that the number of examples does not need to be large — just a few well-chosen examples are enough to guide the model. This is why it is called " few-shot ". The goal is not to retrain the model, but to guide it on the specific task.

[When to use ]
Few-shot Prompting is ideal when the task is complex enough to require additional guidance, but not so complex that it requires extensive training or exhaustive examples. It is suitable for situations where the model needs to understand specific nuances, tones, or shapes, and when there are concrete examples that can be used as reference.

You should use this technique when you need responses that follow a pattern, such as responses that are consistent in format or style, or when there is an ambiguity that needs to be resolved early on. It is especially useful when you want the template to adapt to the style of communication you have in mind—for example, shopping lists, process descriptions, or even dialogues.

Additionally, this technique can be used to tailor LLM responses to the target audience. For example, providing examples that use simpler or more technical language depending on the intended audience is a great way to direct the tone of the response.

[Tips for use]

●        Choose examples that are representative and that actually demonstrate the type of response you want. The closer the example is to the desired task, the better the LLM adaptation will be.

●        Limit the number of examples to a few and keep them concise. You don't need a large number of examples — what matters is the quality and clarity of the examples you provide.

[The difference in technique ]
Few-shot Prompting differs from Zero- shot Prompting because, instead of simply asking the model a direct question, it provides examples to establish a clear pattern. This helps the LLM generate more accurate and expected responses, especially in cases where a direct answer might not be specific enough.

Compared to Chain of Thought , Few-shot Prompting does not necessarily involve a chain of reasoning, but rather the definition of a pattern through examples. While Chain of Thought details the step-by-step reasoning to ensure a logical answer, the Few-shot Prompting focuses more on the formatting and style of the answer, based on specific examples.

Few-shot also differs from Role Play in that it does not instruct the model to assume a specific persona, but rather to follow a specific pattern of questions and answers. Thus, while Role Play helps shape the LLM’s personality, Few-shot helps shape the format of the answer.

[Examples]

●        Basic :

○        Examples Provided :

■        Question: "What is photosynthesis? "
Answer: "Photosynthesis is the process by which plants transform sunlight into energy."

■        Question: "What is gravity? "
Answer: "Gravity is the force that attracts objects to each other."

○        New Question : "What is the water cycle? "

○        Expected Answer : "The water cycle is the process of water circulation between the Earth's surface and the atmosphere. "

●        Intermediary :

○        Examples Provided :

■        Question: "How can I make a simple omelette? "
Answer: "Break two eggs, beat well, season with salt and pepper, then cook in a frying pan with a little oil."

■        Question: "How do I make filtered coffee? "
Answer: "Boil the water, put the coffee powder in the filter and slowly pour the water over the powder."

○        New Question : "How to make herbal tea? "

○        Expected Answer : "Boil water, add herbs, let steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain. "

●        Advanced :

○        Examples Provided :

■        Question: "What are the benefits of meditation? "
Answer: "Meditation can reduce stress, improve concentration, and promote a sense of well-being."

■        Question: "Why is playing sports important? "
Answer: "Playing sports helps improve physical health, reduce stress and increase discipline."

○        New Question : "What are the benefits of a balanced diet? "

○        Expected Answer : "A balanced diet provides necessary nutrients, improves the immune system and maintains adequate energy levels. "

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Few-shot Structured Prompt :

○        {"Question" ["Provide a clear and direct example question to demonstrate the type of question expected"]}

○        {"Answer" ["Provide an answer to the example, ensuring it is clear and concise"] }

○        {"Repeat the pattern" ["Provide more than one example to reinforce the expected response pattern"]}

○        Complete Example :

■        Question: "What is a lunar eclipse? "
Answer: "A lunar eclipse occurs when the Earth is between the Sun and the Moon, blocking sunlight from reaching the Moon."

■        Question: "What is the difference between a meteor and a meteorite? "
Answer: "A meteor is a space rock that burns up as it enters the atmosphere, while a meteorite is what remains after it hits the Earth's surface."

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Provide two sample questions about animals and their answers. Then create a new question about a different animal and ask the model to follow the same answer pattern.
  2. Create an example where the model must explain how to perform two physical activities. Then, ask him to explain another similar physical activity, following the same style as the examples given.
  3. Create two simple recipe examples (question and answer). Ask the model to explain a new recipe following the same pattern and style as the examples provided.

5. Self- Reflective Prompting ( Model Self-Reflection )

[What is ]
Self- Reflective Prompting , or Model Self-Reflection , is a prompt engineering technique that encourages the LLM to reflect on the quality and accuracy of its own response. In this approach, the model is prompted to evaluate itself, correct potential errors, and improve the response before presenting it as final. It is a kind of " self-editing ", which aims to improve the confidence and accuracy of the provided response.

The idea is that the model not only generates a response, but also reviews what you’ve written, looking for inconsistencies or information that might be incorrect. This leads to more accurate interaction, increasing the chance of reliable, quality responses.

[What is your main objective ]
The main objective of Self- Reflective Prompting improves the quality of the response provided by the model by prompting it to review and improve its own reasoning before presenting it as definitive. By making the model reflect on what it has generated, it increases the possibility of identifying errors and adding relevant details that may have been forgotten.

Another goal is to promote more critical and careful reasoning, ensuring that the answer is appropriate, coherent and clear. This technique is especially valuable in contexts where accuracy and consistency are essential, such as when answering complex questions or where there is a need to ensure that the information provided is correct.

[How it works ]
Self- Reflective Prompting works by asking the LLM to critically analyze the response after generating an initial response. The analysis should consider whether the response is clear, complete, and correct. By introducing this “reflection” step, the LLM is encouraged to review the details and, if necessary, make adjustments, improving the final quality of the response.

To apply this technique, the prompt is usually divided into two parts: first, a request for the model to answer the question; and then a request for it to evaluate the answer it just provided. The model should look for gaps or potential problems, such as missing details, inconsistencies, or factual errors.

Additionally, this approach can also be used to encourage deeper reflection on questions of opinion or analysis by having the model consider different perspectives and justify its choices. In this way, the technique can not only improve the answer, but also provide a richer insight into the subject.

[When to use ]
Self- Reflective Prompting is useful when accuracy and quality are a priority. If the question requires a detailed answer or where a mistake could have serious consequences, such as in the medical field, science, or when analyzing a topic in depth, using this technique helps minimize potential errors.

It is also useful when you want the model to provide a more robust answer, since self-reflection prompts the LLM to add details that may not have been included in the initial answer. It is a valuable approach for users who want to get a more detailed or revised result without having to manually revise it.

When dealing with complex, long information or information that can have multiple interpretations, Self- Reflective Prompting can ensure that the model presents the answer in a more structured and less ambiguous way, providing a more reliable and verifiable explanation.

[Tips for use]

●        Make a clear request for the model to review the answer, such as "Please review your answer and correct any errors." The more specific your request for review, the better the result will be.

●        Encourage the model to think about the clarity and completeness of the answer. Questions such as “ Is this answer complete?” or “Is there anything important missing?” can help guide self-reflection .

[The difference of the technique ]
Self- Reflective Prompting is different from other techniques, such as Chain of Thought , as it is not focused on generating a step-by-step explanation right from the start, but rather on reviewing the reasoning already done and improving it. While Chain of Thought aims to guide the thought process from beginning to end, Self- Reflective Prompting applies after an initial response is generated.

Compared to Few-shot Prompting , where examples are provided to guide the format of the response, Self- Reflective Prompting focuses on review. That is, the focus here is not on providing examples, but on ensuring that the model can evaluate and improve its own response.

Unlike Role Play, where the LLM assumes a specific role, Self- Reflective Prompting asks the model to critically evaluate what has been produced, without adopting a persona. This makes this technique effective for improving accuracy and consistency, rather than shaping style or tone.

[Examples]

●        Basic :

○        Question : "What is photosynthesis? "

○        Initial Answer : "Photosynthesis is a process by which plants make food. "

○        Review : "Reviewing my answer, I realize that it is greatly simplified. Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water into glucose and oxygen. "

●        Intermediary :

○        Question : "Explain the benefits of a high protein diet. "

○        Initial Answer : "A high protein diet helps with muscle growth. "

○        Review : "By reviewing, I can expand on the answer. In addition to helping with muscle growth, a high-protein diet also contributes to tissue repair, increased satiety, and maintenance of lean mass during weight loss. "

●        Advanced :

○        Question : "What are the effects of global warming on marine ecosystems? "

○        Initial Response : "Global warming is warming the oceans. "

○        Review : "Upon reviewing, I see that the answer is very generic. Global warming not only increases ocean temperatures, but also causes acidification, impacts coral reefs, changes ocean current patterns and affects marine biodiversity, harming many species. "

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Self- Reflective Structured Prompt :

○        {"Question" ["Request a question or task for LLM to answer"]}

○        {"Initial Response" ["Allow LLM to generate the initial response based on its knowledge"]}

○        {" Self-reflection " ["Ask the LLM to review their own response and look for inconsistencies or areas for improvement"]}

○        Complete Example :

■        Question: "What is the role of the liver in the human body? "

■        Initial Answer: "The liver helps in the digestion of food. "

■        Self-reflection : "Reviewing my answer, I see that it is incomplete. The liver also filters toxins from the blood, produces proteins important for clotting, and stores vitamins and minerals. "

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Ask the model a question, such as "What is biodiversity?", and ask it to provide an answer, then revise that answer to improve clarity and depth.
  2. Instruct the model to answer the question "How does the water cycle work?" and then to review its answer, adding details and correcting any possible errors.
  3. Ask the model to explain "Why is it important to recycle?" and ask them to review the answer, ensuring it is complete and without any important omissions.

6. Instruction-based Prompting ( Instruction-Based Prompting )

[What is ]
Instruction-based Prompting , or Instruction-Based Prompting , is a prompt engineering technique where the LLM is given specific and detailed instructions on what to do, how to respond, and what information to include or exclude. In this method, the model is guided step-by-step with explicit instructions that clearly guide it through the requested task.

This technique is useful to ensure that the answer provided follows the user's directions exactly, without ambiguity. The LLM receives clear guidelines, such as "explain briefly", "list only the benefits", or "use real-life examples".

[What is your main objective ]
The main objective of Instruction-based Prompting is about getting consistent and accurate responses by shaping the LLM output to ensure it follows the instructions given exactly. This helps reduce uncertainty and make the model's behavior more predictable, even across varied topics.

Another goal is to improve the efficiency of the LLM’s response by guiding it in how it should process and structure the information. Detailed instructions can help it achieve exactly what is expected, whether it is a short, straightforward explanation or a long, detailed analysis. This is useful for achieving different levels of complexity as needed.

[How it works ]
Instruction-based Prompting works by breaking the request into clear, specific parts. The user gives the LLM a series of detailed instructions that it must follow to generate the expected response. The instructions can specify the tone (formal, informal), style (explanatory, enumerative), and the ), or even the content (include examples, avoid jargon, etc.).

For example, the user might ask, "Explain the process of photosynthesis in a short sentence and without using technical terms." This specific instruction guides the model on how to generate the answer. The directions provided to the LLM help to avoid answers that are too broad, imprecise, or out of the desired context.

This technique requires that the instructions be clear and direct. The more detailed the instructions are, the more likely the LLM will be able to follow exactly what is being asked. This approach may include not only the content of the answer, but also how it should be presented, e.g., "list in bullet points", "use examples from everyday use", etc.

[When to use ]
Instruction-based Prompting is useful when you have a clear and specific goal for your LLM response. If you need a response that follows a specific format or style, such as a list, a step-by-step guide, or even a short, to-the-point analysis, this technique is ideal.

It is also recommended in situations where there is a lot of possible variability in the response. By providing precise instructions, the model is guided to respond in a specific way, which helps ensure consistency, especially in tasks where nuances can lead to misinterpretation.

For example, in educational contexts, when it is important that the answer is adapted to the student's level of understanding, or in work contexts where specific information and directions need to be followed, this technique is ideal for ensuring clarity and accuracy.

[Tips for use]

●        Be as specific as possible when providing instructions. The more details and directions you give , the more the answer will align with your expectations.

●        Instruct the model about the type of content, level of depth, or tone of the response, such as "answer in simple, child-friendly language" or "provide a technical analysis using specific terms."

[The difference of the technique ]
Instruction-based Prompting differs from Zero- shot Prompting because, instead of simply asking for a direct answer, it includes detailed directions on how the answer should be constructed. This helps to avoid vague or off-topic answers.

Compared to Few-shot Prompting , where the LLM is guided through previous examples, in Instruction-based Prompting focuses on directing the model's behavior with clear instructions, without the need to provide previous examples. This is most effective when you need a response that follows a single, direct style, without the need to train the model with previous examples.

Furthermore, unlike Role Play, where LLM assumes a role or persona, Instruction-based Prompting focuses on how the response should be worded rather than on shaping the personality of the subject. This makes it a straightforward technique for ensuring that the response meets the required criteria.

[Examples]

●        Basic :

○        Question : "What is the water cycle? Explain it in a short, simple sentence. "

○        Answer : "The water cycle is the movement of water from the surface to the atmosphere and back again, through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation. "

●        Intermediary :

○        Question : "List the main benefits of a plant-rich diet. Use informal and clear language. "

○        Response :

■        "1. Helps maintain heart health.

  1. Improves digestion.
  2. Provides essential vitamins and minerals.
  3. It can help control weight."

●        Advanced :

○        Question : "Explain the effects of air pollution on human health, but do not use medical jargon. Provide two examples of the impact on everyday life. "

○        Answer : "Air pollution can cause breathing problems and make people more tired. For example, someone may feel short of breath when taking a simple walk, and children may have more coughing fits when pollution is high. "

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Instruction-based Structured Prompt :

○        {"Question/Task" ["Provide the main question or request to the model"]}

○        {"Specific instructions" ["Include clear directions, such as: 'Explain in one short sentence', 'List the main points', 'Use everyday examples'"] }

○        {"Request for details" ["If necessary, include additional requests such as tone, depth of response, etc."] }

○        Complete Example :

■        Question: "How does the circulatory system work? Explain in simple terms and in no more than two sentences. "

■        Instruction: "Use accessible language, suitable for elementary school students. "

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Give the model the task of explaining "How Plants Grow" and include the instruction to use simple, jargon-free terms in three sentences or less.
  2. Ask the model to list "Three benefits of physical activity" using motivational language aimed at beginners in physical exercise.
  3. Instruct the model to explain "What is a solar eclipse?" and provide an additional instruction: "Describe the phenomenon in scientific language, but one that can be understood by high school students. "

7. Reframing

[What is ]
Reframing , or Reframing, is a prompt engineering technique that involves reformulating the problem or question presented to the LLM so that it is seen from a new perspective. The goal is to help the model respond differently, offering a more comprehensive or in-depth view of the same topic, depending on the approach taken.

In other words, Reframing is a way of guiding the model to consider a different aspect of the initial question by changing the way the prompt is asked. This might include rephrasing the prompt , inverting the point of view, or asking the model to consider other contexts and angles.

[What is your main goal ?]
The main goal of Reframing is to obtain a richer and more diverse answer by exploring different facets of the same question. Often, a direct question can generate a limited answer; by reframing the problem, the LLM is encouraged to analyze and provide more creative or complete answers.

Another goal is to address situations where the model appears to have misunderstood the initial question or provided an inadequate answer. Reframing the question gives the model a new opportunity to interpret the problem in a different way and bring in information that was not considered in the original answer.

[How it works ]
Reframing works by rephrasing the initial prompt so that the model has a different perspective on the question. This can be done in a variety of ways, such as changing the tone of the prompt , adding or removing details, or even asking the model how someone else would respond. Reframing changes the context and approach to expand the type of response generated.

For example, instead of asking directly, “What are the benefits of exercise?” you could reframe the question as, “How does exercise contribute to mental and physical health?” or “Why do many people recommend exercise as part of a healthy lifestyle?” These reframes allow the model to consider different aspects of the same question.

Additionally, Reframing is a useful technique when the generated answer does not meet expectations. By modifying the way the question is asked, the model is encouraged to take a new angle, often resulting in a more relevant answer. This is especially useful when trying to get an answer that is more comprehensive or detailed.

[When to use ]
Reframing should be used when the answer initially provided seems limited or does not explore the topic in a satisfactory way. If the answer does not meet your expectations, reframing the question can help you get a new result that is more aligned with what you are looking for. This technique is ideal for situations where you want to explore different perspectives on a topic.

It is also a valuable technique in creative contexts, such as idea development or brainstorming. By reframing the question, the model can provide different approaches to the same idea, helping in the process of creating and exploring new possibilities.

Additionally, Reframing is useful when working with complex topics that may be difficult to understand at first. By changing the angle of the issue, it becomes possible to explore the nuances of the problem, causing the model to bring out different elements and parts of the topic, making it easier to understand.

[Tips for use]

●        Try rephrasing the question using different words or phrases. A small change can drastically change the model's perspective.

●        Consider changing the tone of the request. For example, if you’re asking for a detailed analysis, try reframing the question to ask for an opinion or summary, and vice versa.

[The difference in technique ]
Reframing is different from Zero- shot Prompting and Few-shot Prompting , because instead of asking directly or providing examples, it reframes the question to explore new responses. While Zero- shot and Few-shot focus on the initial prompt and examples given, Reframing changes the question itself to encourage a diverse approach.

Compared to Role Play, which involves asking the LLM to take on a specific role, Reframing focuses on changing the perspective on the issue without necessarily changing the role of the model. Instead of trying to make the model respond as someone else, it simply changes the way they see the problem.

In the context of Chain of Thought , where the focus is on creating a detailed line of reasoning, Reframing is not necessarily concerned with explaining step by step, but rather with generating new perspectives. The idea is to broaden the understanding of the issue, and not detail the reasoning behind a single perspective.

[Examples]

●        Basic :

○        Original Question : "What is a solar eclipse? "

○        Reframed : "How does a solar eclipse occur and what effect does it have on Earth? "

○        Answer : "A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon moves between the Earth and the Sun, blocking sunlight for a short period of time. This creates a shadow on the Earth's surface and can temporarily dim certain areas. "

●        Intermediary :

○        Original Question : "What are the benefits of recycling? "

○        Reframed : "Why is recycling important for the environment and for people? "

○        Answer : "Recycling is important because it helps reduce the amount of waste in landfills, saves natural resources, and reduces pollution. It also promotes environmental awareness and can create economic opportunities for communities. "

●        Advanced :

○        Original Question : "Explain the importance of the theory of evolution. "

○        Reframed : "How has the theory of evolution contributed to our understanding of biology and the diversity of life? "

○        Answer : "The theory of evolution has provided a basis for understanding how species adapt and change over time. It explains the diversity of life and the processes of natural selection, which is essential for studies in genetics, ecology, and even medicine. "

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Reframing Structured Prompt :

○        {"Original Question" ["Please provide the main question or request"]}

○        {"Reframe" ["Reframe the question to change the point of view, add details, or expand the scope"] }

○        {"Improved Response" ["Use the new perspective to generate a response that explores new elements or provides more detail"]}

○        Complete Example :

■        Original Question: "What is the function of leaves in plants? "

■        Reframing: "How do leaves contribute to plant survival and how do they perform their main functions? "

■        Improved Answer: "Leaves are responsible for photosynthesis, the process that converts sunlight into energy for the plant. They also help with gas exchange, absorbing carbon dioxide and releasing oxygen, and regulate water loss through transpiration. "

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Take the question “What is a cell?” and reframe it to “How do cells function as the basic unit of life?” Ask the model to answer from this new perspective.
  2. Reframe the question “Why are bees important?” to “How do bees affect the environment and our agriculture?” and see how the model answers with a broader view.
  3. Ask “What causes climate change?” and then reframe it to “What are the effects of climate change on human populations?” to explore the impact of the problem from a new angle.

8. Context Expansion (Context Expansion)

[What is ]
Context Expansion , or Context Expansion, is a prompt engineering technique that consists of providing the LLM with a broader or more detailed context about the subject before asking the main question. The goal is to ensure that the model better understands the full scenario, allowing a more accurate and informed answer.

Context expansion works by adding relevant information before you directly ask the LLM. This can include background, additional descriptions, term definitions, or examples that help the model better understand the question. Instead of asking a question directly, you set the stage by creating a richer scenario for the LLM to interpret.

[What is your main goal ?]
The main goal of Context Expansion is to improve the relevance and accuracy of the LLM's answer by providing him with all the information he needs to fully understand the question. This is especially important when the question is complex or relies on specific details that, without proper context, could be misinterpreted.

Another goal is to ensure that the LLM responds appropriately to a specific target audience. By expanding the context, you can inform the LLM about expectations and the type of language to be used, such as whether the response should be technical or simple, or whether it should be geared toward children or experts.

[How it works ]
Context Expansion works by adding introductory information before the main question. This information helps to "set up" the model, providing a complete picture of the topic. This way, the LLM has more elements to consider when responding, and, consequently, generates a more aligned and detailed answer.

For example, before asking, “What are the advantages of solar energy?” you could expand on the context by saying, “Given that solar energy is a renewable form of electricity, especially important in areas where there is a lot of sunlight throughout the year, what are the advantages of this type of energy?” By providing more detail before the main question, the LLM is guided to consider these points in their answer.

Another example of how this works would be if you were explaining a concept to a child. Before asking, “What is gravity?” you might say, “Imagine that you are holding a ball and you drop it. The ball falls to the ground because of a force called gravity. Now, what is gravity?” This type of approach creates a scenario where the LLM understands that the answer should be child-friendly.

[When to use ]
The Context Expansion technique should be used when the problem being addressed is complex or requires prior knowledge in order to provide a correct answer. Situations where a simple answer would be insufficient also benefit from this technique, because by providing additional context, the LLM can be guided to generate a more complete and informative answer.

This technique is useful when dealing with different audiences, such as explaining a scientific concept to a lay audience or a child. By providing appropriate context, the model can adapt the level of response to the audience’s needs.

Additionally, it is recommended in cases where the LLM initially provided an incomplete or incorrect answer, as by providing more context, it is possible to redirect the model's focus to the important and necessary points, helping it to avoid mistakes.

[Tips for use]

●        Include background information that is relevant, but avoid overdoing it with context. The goal is to be straightforward and provide only the information necessary to guide the model.

●        When expanding the context, think about who will read the response. For example, adapt the level of detail and type of language according to your intended audience.

[The difference in technique ]
Context Expansion differs from Zero- shot and Few-shot Prompting because, instead of just providing examples or asking directly, it expands the information base before making the request. This gives the model more elements to generate a response aligned with what is expected.

Compared to Role Play, where the LLM assumes a specific role or perspective, in Context Expansion the focus is on expanding the scenario in which the question will be asked. The model is not "playing" a specific character, but is receiving additional information to better understand the context.

In relation to Reframing , where the goal is to change the perspective of a question, Context Expansion is concerned with giving more information to the model before asking the main question. It does not change the angle of the question, but rather the amount of information available for the model to give a better answer.

[Examples]

●        Basic :

○        Expanded Context : "Solar energy is one of the most widely used renewable energy sources, especially in sunny regions. It converts sunlight directly into electricity through solar panels. "

○        Question : "What are the main benefits of solar energy? "

○        Answer : "The main benefits of solar energy are that it is a clean, non-polluting and renewable source of energy, as well as reducing dependence on fossil fuels. "

●        Intermediary :

○        Expanded Context : "The oceans cover about 70% of the Earth's surface and play a crucial role in regulating the climate and sustaining marine life. "

○        Question : "How do the oceans contribute to regulating the planet's climate? "

○        Answer : "The oceans absorb large amounts of heat from the atmosphere, helping to regulate global temperature. In addition, they are responsible for releasing water vapor, which influences the rainfall cycle. "

●        Advanced :

○        Expanded Context : "In Einstein's theory of relativity, space and time are interdependent and form a fabric that can be deformed by the presence of large masses, such as planets and stars. "

○        Question : "How does the theory of relativity explain gravity? "

○        Answer : "The theory of relativity explains gravity as the curvature of the fabric of space-time around a massive body. Smaller objects move along this curvature, which we perceive as the gravitational force. "

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Context Expansion Structured Prompt :

○        {"Expanded Context" ["Provide introductory information or additional context about the topic, setting the stage for the main question"] }

○        {"Main Question" ["Make the request specific, ensuring that the context has already provided the basis for an informed answer"] }

○        {"Response" ["LLM uses context to respond accurately and in detail"]}

○        Complete Example :

■        Expanded Context: "Photosynthesis is the fundamental process by which plants produce energy using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide. "

■        Main Question: "How does photosynthesis benefit the ecosystem as a whole? "

■        Answer: "Photosynthesis not only produces glucose for the plant, but also releases oxygen, which is essential for the respiration of other living beings, helping to maintain the balance of ecosystems. "

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Give the model the context "Recycling is an important process for solid waste management, reducing the amount of waste that goes to landfills." and ask the model to explain "What are the positive impacts of recycling on the environment?"
  2. Give the model the context "Pollinating animals, such as bees, play a vital role in the reproduction of many plants, ensuring food production." and then ask "Why is protecting bees crucial for agriculture?"
  3. Provide context "Artificial intelligence is increasingly present in our daily lives, from virtual assistants to medical diagnostics." and ask "What are the benefits and challenges of applying artificial intelligence in medicine?"

9. Elimination

[What is ]
Elimination , or Elimination, is a prompt engineering technique that consists of explicitly limiting the information or perspectives that should not be included in the LLM response. The focus is on filtering out irrelevant information or eliminating unwanted approaches, so that the model generates a response aligned with what really matters.

The elimination technique involves asking the LLM to disregard certain elements, terms, or details while crafting the response. This approach is especially useful for narrowing the scope of the response and ensuring that the content is highly focused and free of distractions or ambiguities.

[What is your main goal ?]
The main goal of Elimination is to increase the relevance and clarity of the LLM response by removing from the context any type of noise or elements that could lead to an overly broad or scattered response. Often, the model may include information that, although correct, is not useful for the user's specific purpose. The elimination technique helps to avoid this.

Another goal is to make your answer more efficient and to the point. This is especially important in situations where there is a lot of information on a topic, but you just want a focused, specific answer. The technique can also be used to avoid certain angles that you don’t want, such as eliminating scientific jargon when your audience is a layperson.

[How it works ]
Elimination works by including a prompt in the prompt explicitly indicating which elements should be avoided. The user instructs the LLM not to mention or consider certain aspects, which narrows the scope of the response. This may include terms, topics, examples, or language styles that should not be used.

For example, if you’re looking for a simplified explanation of gravity for a child, you might ask the LLM to: “Explain what gravity is, but don’t use complex scientific terms.” Another example might be: “What are the benefits of a vegetarian diet, but don’t mention environmental aspects.” This type of prompt ensures that the answer is focused on exactly what the user needs, without distracting information.

This technique can also be useful for avoiding information that may be sensitive or not pertinent to the context. This helps to keep the response appropriate for the specific audience and avoids unwanted information, making the interaction more controlled.

[When to use ]
Elimination should be used when the topic has a lot of possible information, but you need an answer focused on only one specific aspect. This is useful, for example, when explaining complex concepts to an audience that doesn't need technical details, or to keep the answer aligned with a specific purpose.

It is recommended when you want to eliminate ambiguity. Often, the LLM can include a long answer that addresses all aspects of a topic. If you are interested in just one perspective or in quick, direct answers, using Elimination helps you limit the information to what is truly relevant.

This technique is also effective when you need to adapt your answer to the audience’s level of understanding. For example, when explaining a scientific concept to children, you might ask them not to use technical terms or omit complex details.

[Tips for use]

●        Be specific about what should be avoided. The clearer you are about what should not be included, the more focused the LLM’s response will be.

●        Combine Elimination with other techniques to increase accuracy. For example, provide context (context expansion) and then eliminate aspects that are not relevant to the scenario presented.

[The difference of the technique ]
Elimination differs from other techniques such as Context Expansion , because while context expansion adds information to enrich the answer, elimination removes certain elements to narrow the scope. This makes the answer targeted to the necessary point, eliminating irrelevant elements.

Compared to Reframing , which changes the perspective of the question to explore a new point of view, Elimination simply removes unwanted information from the context of the answer. It is a technique that aims to reduce the scope and avoid details that are not useful, while Reframing tries to change the angle of the problem.

Regarding Instruction-based Prompting , where specific instructions are given about the format of the answer, Elimination is focused on ensuring what should not be included, while Instruction-based Prompting focuses on how the response should be structured. Elimination aims to ensure that the response is accurate by avoiding distracting elements, while detail-based instructions guide what should be done.

[Examples]

●        Basic :

○        Question : "What is the greenhouse effect? "

○        Deletion Instruction : "Explain what the greenhouse effect is, but do not mention specific gases. "

○        Answer : "The greenhouse effect is the process by which the atmosphere traps heat from the Sun, keeping the Earth warm enough to sustain life. "

●        Intermediary :

○        Question : "What are the benefits of a vegetarian diet? "

○        Deletion Instruction : "List the benefits, but do not mention the environmental impact. "

○        Answer : "A vegetarian diet can help improve heart health, control weight, reduce the risk of certain chronic diseases, and provide nutrition rich in fiber and vitamins. "

●        Advanced :

○        Question : "What are the impacts of climate change on cities? "

○        Deletion Instruction : "Explain the impacts, but do not mention natural disasters. "

○        Answer : "Climate change affects cities by increasing stress on infrastructure, creating challenges for water supplies, increasing energy costs for cooling, and increasing the risk of public health problems due to extreme heat. "

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Elimination Structured Prompt :

○        {"Question/Task" ["Provide the main question or request to the model"]}

○        {"Elimination Instruction" ["Include directions on what to avoid in the answer"]}

○        {"Response" ["LLM generates the response based on the request, avoiding the indicated elements"] }

○        Complete Example :

■        Question: "What is a solar eclipse? "

■        Deletion Instruction: "Explain simply, but do not mention the term ' umbra '. "

■        Answer: "A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon passes between the Sun and Earth, blocking the Sun's light for a brief period. "

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Ask the model to explain "What is the food chain?" but instruct him or her not to mention examples of specific animals. Evaluate how the model stays focused without these examples.
  2. Ask the model to explain “What are the benefits of solar energy?”, eliminating any mention of costs or financial savings. Notice how the answer changes, focusing only on environmental or sustainability aspects.
  3. Ask, “How important is water to the human body?” but eliminate any mention of water’s role in the digestion process. See how the answer is shaped without including that specific aspect.

10. Goal Decomposition (Goal Decomposition)

[What is ]
Goal Decomposition , or Goal Decomposition, is a prompt engineering technique that consists of breaking down a complex request into smaller, more manageable steps. This technique helps the LLM deal with questions that involve multiple interrelated tasks or parts, facilitating the response process by decomposing the overall goal into multiple sub-goals .

The central idea is to take a larger task and break it down into logical, organized parts, guiding the LLM to solve them one by one until it reaches the complete solution. With this approach, the smaller parts can be more easily understood and answered, resulting in a more accurate and well-structured overall answer.

[What is your main objective ]
The main objective of the Goal Decomposition deals with tasks that may be too complex to be answered in one go. By breaking down the problem, the LLM can focus on solving each subpart with clarity and precision, increasing the quality of the final answer. This is particularly useful when the task has multiple steps or aspects that need to be addressed separately.

Another important goal is to foster a deeper understanding of how to achieve the end goal. Breaking down the goal helps the model to walk through each of the necessary steps, avoiding logical leaps and providing a more understandable and detailed answer. This technique is essential to avoid generic and simplified answers when dealing with topics that require more in-depth analysis.

[How it works ]
Goal Decomposition works by asking the LLM to break down the task into multiple steps. Initially, the user can ask the LLM to identify the subtasks needed to complete the overall goal. Then, each of these subtasks can be answered individually. This process can be guided with prompts that encourage logical division and planning of the steps to be followed.

For example, if you ask the model to explain “How do I develop a mobile app?”, an example of goal decomposition would be to break the answer into the following parts: “Define the app idea”, “Choose the programming language”, “Design the user interface”, “ Implement the features”, and “Test the app”. This way, the LLM can answer each part in sequence, providing a clear view of the entire process.

This approach is ideal when there are multiple variables involved in solving a problem. The process of breaking the objective into smaller parts allows the LLM to focus on each of the individual components before integrating them into the overall solution.

[When to use ]
Goal Decomposition should be used when the request involves a task that may be too broad or complex to be answered in one go. This technique is useful for solving problems that involve multiple steps or require detailed explanations that cover several aspects of the topic.

It is ideal for situations that require planning, such as when the goal is to create something, organize complex information, or solve a problem that has several layers. The technique is also widely used in educational contexts, as it allows students to see the process step by step, helping them understand the whole concept.

Furthermore, Goal Decomposition is recommended when the user needs an answer that includes all the important details, without missing any aspects along the way. By breaking down the objective, the LLM can address each aspect in detail and coherently, avoiding omissions.

[Tips for use]

●        When asking your LLM to break down an objective, first ask for a list of sub-tasks and then ask them to explain each one. This will ensure that each step is understood before it is detailed.

●        Be specific when asking the model to explain how a goal can be broken down. Tell the model to focus on “breaking it down into simple, logical steps,” and then ask the model to expand on each step.

[The difference in technique ]
Goal Decomposition differs from Chain of Thought , which focuses on thinking through steps to reason about a problem, but usually aims to arrive at a single specific answer. Goal Decomposition , on the other hand, involves creating multiple sub-tasks that need to be completed to achieve a larger goal. The Chain of Thought is more of a continuous logical line, while Goal Decomposition breaks the task into discrete parts.

Compared to Few-shot Prompting , where examples are provided to the model so that it understands the expected response, decomposition focuses on breaking the original request into several smaller parts. While Few-shot helps shape the style of the response, Goal Decomposition deals with the complexity of content.

Furthermore, unlike Instruction-based Prompting , which gives specific instructions on how to respond, Goal Decomposition guides the model to create a structured plan to respond to the objective, addressing distinct parts in sequence. Instead of focusing on how the response should be structured, it focuses on how the problem should be broken down.

[Examples]

●        Basic :

○        Question : "How to plant a tree in the garden? "

○        Breakdown of Objectives :

■        Choosing the right tree for your climate.

■        Prepare the soil for planting.

■        Dig a suitable hole.

■        Place the tree and cover with soil.

■        Water and care for the tree after planting.

○        Response :

■        1. Choose the right tree for your climate : Choose a species that adapts well to your region's climate to ensure healthy growth.

■        2. Prepare the soil for planting : Make sure the soil is well-drained and add fertilizer if necessary.

■        (continues detailing each step).

●        Intermediary :

○        Question : "How to plan an international trip? "

○        Breakdown of Objectives :

■        Set the destination and dates.

■        Make a budget for the trip.

■        Book flights and accommodation.

■        Plan the tourist itinerary.

■        Prepare documents and luggage.

○        Response :

■        1. Define the destination and dates : Choose the destination considering your interests and the time of year you can travel.

■        2. Make a travel budget : Estimate the costs of transportation, accommodation, food and tours.

■        (continue with the other steps).

●        Advanced :

○        Question : "How to develop a business plan for a technology startup?"

○        Breakdown of Objectives :

■        Define the value proposition and the problem that will be solved.

■        Analyze the target market and competition.

■        Develop the business model.

■        Plan marketing and sales strategies.

■        Establish a financial plan with projections.

○        Response :

■        1. Define the value proposition and the problem that will be solved : The first step is to understand what problem your startup is solving and what value proposition it brings to customers.

■        2. Analyze the target market and competition : Evaluate the target audience and carry out a detailed analysis of the competition, identifying opportunities.

■        (continue with the other steps).

[Structure of the technique used above]

●        Goal Decomposition Structured Prompt :

○        {"Question/Task" ["Provide the main question or request to the model"]}

○        {"Goal Breakdown" ["Ask the model to break down the goal into clear, logical subtasks "]}

○        {"Answers to Subtasks " ["Ask the model to provide answers to each of the steps in a detailed and sequential manner"]}

○        Complete Example :

■        Question: "How to organize a corporate event? "

■        Breakdown of Objectives:

■        Determine the purpose of the event.

■        Choose the date and location.

■        Plan the budget.

■        Make the guest list.

■        Hire suppliers.

■        Response:

■        1. Determine the purpose of the event : Understand what the purpose of the event is — networking, training, celebration — so that all planning revolves around that purpose.

■        (continues detailing each sub-task ).

Prompt exercises using the above technique]

  1. Ask the model to explain "How to write a novel?" by breaking the goal down into several parts and describing each one.
  2. Ask the model to detail "How to open a small clothing store?", breaking the task down into clear steps, such as choosing a location, financial planning, purchasing inventory, among others.
  3. Instruct the model to explain “How to learn a new language?” by breaking down the goals into smaller parts, such as setting learning goals, choosing resources, and daily practices.

11. Step-by-Step Detailing (Step-by-Step Detailing)

[What is ]
Step-by-Step Detailing , or Step-by-Step Detailing, is a prompt engineering technique in which the LLM is guided to describe a process or respond to a request by providing each step in detail, one at a time. The goal is for the model to lead the explanation in a clear, guided, and organized way, to facilitate the understanding of complex or multi-stage processes.

This technique is useful for breaking down large blocks of information into smaller, easily digestible chunks, providing a logical and intuitive sequence for the reader. The step-by-step breakdown aims to ensure that no aspect of the process is overlooked and that each step is presented in an understandable way.

[What is your main objective ]
The main objective of Step-by-Step Detailing is about ensuring clarity and completeness of the answer. When faced with tasks that have multiple steps or that might be difficult to understand if presented all at once, step-by-step detailing makes it easier to understand by providing specific instructions in small chunks.

Another important goal is to avoid confusion or omissions. In many cases, a general answer can be too broad and leave out important details. By detailing each step of the process, the LLM is guided to not skip any steps, providing an exhaustive answer that covers all relevant details.

[How it works ]
Step-by-Step Detailing works by directing the LLM to describe the answer sequentially, in numbered or listed steps. The prompt should include words such as "step-by-step", "detail each step" or "describe each phase" to encourage the model to break the answer down into smaller, more explanatory parts.

For example, if you ask, "How do I make a cake recipe?" you might specify, "Explain the step-by-step process from preparing the ingredients to finishing." LLM will then break the answer down into steps such as, "Step 1 : Gather all the necessary ingredients," "Step 2: Preheat the oven," and so on.

This technique is particularly effective when the user needs to follow specific instructions to perform an action. Whether it’s cooking, assembling a piece of equipment, or solving a math problem, step-by-step detail provides a clear guide to follow.

[When to use ]
Step-by-Step Detailing is useful when you need an organized and clear answer about how to perform a complex task. This technique is ideal for tutorials, instruction manuals, scientific explanations, or any other situation that involves a series of actions that must be followed.

It is recommended in situations where you want to avoid confusion by ensuring that the LLM provides a structured answer where each step can be understood in isolation. It is especially suitable for audiences who are learning about a topic for the first time and who need precise guidance.

Furthermore, Step-by-Step Detailing can be used in contexts where a logical solution is required. Breaking down the problem into steps allows the LLM to construct the reasoning in a structured manner, which is important for mathematical or logical problems, ensuring that each step is addressed and solved appropriately.

[Tips for use]

●        Use terms that encourage breaking down the answer into steps, such as "step-by-step," "number each step," or "explain each part separately."

●        Ask that the model be specific at each step, providing details about each action, and that it does not skip any part of the process.

[The difference of the technique ]
Step-by-Step Detailing differs from Goal Decomposition in which, although both techniques involve breaking a problem down into parts, Step-by-Step Breakdown focuses on guiding the reader through a process in a linear fashion, one step after another, while Goal Decomposition can simply describe the elements that make up the objective, without necessarily guiding them in a linear sequence.
